questions.question: Why can't a Twitter timeline just be generated with one query at page-load time at scale?
options: SQL doesn't support ORDER BY
The workload is extremely read-heavy and the follow graph is hugely skewed, so merging posts from all followees per load is too slow at hundreds of millions of users
Timelines must be stored on the client
Queries are illegal at scale
questions.explanation: At scale, timeline reads vastly outnumber posts and users follow thousands of accounts (some followed by 100M+). Running a merge query across all followees on every page-load is far too slow, so the feed must be precomputed and/or cached — and celebrities make naive precomputation explode.

questions.question: What does 'fan-out on write' (push) mean for a news feed?
options: Reading the feed by querying every followee at read time
When a user posts, immediately pushing the post into every follower's precomputed timeline
Writing the post to disk once and never copying it
Deleting old posts on write
questions.explanation: Fan-out on write pushes each new post into all followers' precomputed timelines at post time, so reads become a trivial lookup of a ready-made list. The cost is expensive writes — and a catastrophic one for users with huge follower counts.

questions.question: What is the 'celebrity problem' in fan-out-on-write?
options: Celebrities post too rarely
A user with 100M followers triggers 100M timeline inserts per post — a write-amplification bomb that overwhelms the system
Celebrities can't be followed
Their tweets are too long
questions.explanation: Pushing every celebrity post into 100M+ follower timelines means one post causes 100M writes, which can take minutes and overwhelm the system. This write amplification is why pure fan-out-on-write fails for high-follower accounts.

questions.question: How does the hybrid fan-out approach resolve the celebrity problem?
options: It bans celebrities
Push for normal users (cheap fan-out, instant reads); for celebrities, don't fan out on write — store once and merge their recent posts in at read time
It uses pure fan-out-on-read for everyone
It caches the entire database
questions.explanation: The hybrid splits by follower count: normal users' posts are pushed to followers' precomputed feeds, while celebrities' posts are stored once and pulled in at read time (only a few celebrities to merge per user). This caps write fan-out and keeps reads fast.

questions.question: Why is eventual consistency appropriate for a news feed?
options: Feeds require the strongest possible consistency
A tweet appearing a few seconds late harms no one, so trading strict freshness for availability and low latency is the right call
Because feeds never change
Because consistency models don't apply to feeds
questions.explanation: A feed is a perfect fit for eventual consistency: nobody is harmed if a new tweet shows up a couple seconds later. Demanding strong consistency would hurt availability and latency for no real benefit, so the feed converges quickly rather than blocking.
